serving.description: 3 pieces of peanut glutinous rice ball tangyuan in cold soy milk
serving.natural_language_portion: One serving consists of 3 peanut-filled glutinous rice balls (approximately 90–105 g total) served in approximately 240–250 ml of cold soy milk, totalling approximately 350 g.

nutrient_source.title: Tangyuan (glutinous rice ball with peanut filling) and unsweetened soy milk — component-based estimation using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient data
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (FoodData Central) and Health Promotion Board Singapore (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for peanut tangyuan in cold soy milk as a composite dish. Nutrients estimated by component: glutinous rice ball dough (glutinous rice flour, water) from USDA FoodData Central NDB 20052; peanut-sugar filling from USDA FoodData Central NDB 16087 (peanut butter, smooth) and sugar; cold soy milk from HPB Singapore Foodo soy milk entry and USDA FoodData Central NDB 16222. Proportions estimated as approximately 30% glutinous rice dough, 10% peanut filling, 60% soy milk by weight. Values represent a blended per-100g estimate of the composite dish.

gi_source.title: Glutinous rice products and soy milk GI values — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for peanut tangyuan in soy milk as a composite dish. GI estimated as a weighted blend: glutinous rice ball dough GI ~65 (glutinous rice, University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008, Table entry for glutinous rice); peanut filling GI ~14 (peanuts, University of Sydney GI Database); soy milk GI ~34 (soy milk, University of Sydney GI Database). Weighted by carbohydrate contribution: rice dough ~55%, peanut filling ~15%, soy milk ~30%. Blended GI ≈ (0.55×65) + (0.15×14) + (0.30×34) = 35.75 + 2.1 + 10.2 = 48. GI category: low (≤55). GL per serving = GI/100 × carbohydrate_g_per_serving = 48/100 × 49.7 g carbohydrate (350g × 14.2g/100g) = 23.9, rounded to 23.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (~133 mg per serving) from soy milk; no added salt in traditional preparation
hypertension.guidance: With approximately 133 mg sodium per serving, this dish is relatively low in sodium and can be consumed occasionally by individuals with hypertension, provided overall daily sodium intake remains within the recommended limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~24) driven by glutinous rice dough; peanut filling and soy milk moderate the glycemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated glycemic load of approximately 24 per serving is moderate; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 1–2 rice balls and pair with unsweetened soy milk to reduce the overall postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~1.75 g per serving), zero cholesterol, and beneficial unsaturated fats from peanuts and soy
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish provides predominantly unsaturated fats from peanuts and soy milk with negligible saturated fat and zero cholesterol, making it a heart-friendly dessert option when consumed in moderate portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Peanut Glutinous Rice Ball Tangyuan in Cold Soy Milk is a traditional Chinese dessert dish popular in Singapore, consisting of three chewy glutinous rice balls (tangyuan, 汤圆) filled with a sweet ground peanut and sugar paste, served submerged in cold, creamy soy milk. The rice balls are made from glutinous rice flour dough, which is hand-rolled, filled with the peanut mixture, and boiled until they float, then chilled and placed in cold soy milk. The texture is soft, chewy, and slightly sticky from the glutinous rice skin, with a rich, nutty, and mildly sweet peanut filling that contrasts pleasantly with the cool, lightly sweet soy milk base. Culturally, tangyuan are associated with the Lantern Festival (元宵节) and Winter Solstice (冬至) celebrations in Chinese tradition, symbolising family reunion and togetherness. In Singapore, this dish is served year-round at Chinese dessert stalls and noodle restaurants. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate energy (~343 kcal per serving), moderate carbohydrates from glutinous rice, plant-based protein and unsaturated fats from peanuts and soy milk, and negligible saturated fat and cholesterol. The estimated glycemic index is approximately 48 (low GI), though the glycemic load per serving is moderate (~24) due to the carbohydrate-dense glutinous rice dough. It is suitable for vegetarians and vegans, is dairy-free, and is halal-friendly. Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should moderate portion size due to the moderate glycemic load.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish (peanut tangyuan in cold soy milk). Nutrient values are estimated from component-level data using USDA FoodData Central and HPB Singapore Foodo for soy milk. GI is a weighted analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database values for glutinous rice, peanuts, and soy milk. Confidence is medium rather than low because all component sources are approved primary sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated; no single approved-source entry exists for this exact composite dish. Estimation proportions: ~30% glutinous rice dough, ~10% peanut filling, ~60% soy milk by weight. 2. Serving size of 350 g is estimated (3 rice balls ~100 g + ~250 ml cold soy milk). 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component sources used. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo soy milk entry and is below reliable detection in the glutinous rice component. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not consistently reported across component sources. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo soy milk entry. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as no hydrogenated fats are used in traditional preparation. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as all ingredients are plant-based. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as no vitamin C-containing ingredients are present. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as no vitamin D-containing ingredients are present. 5. GI estimated as weighted blend of glutinous rice GI ~65, peanut GI ~14, soy milk GI ~34 per University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008). gi_category is low (GI 48 ≤ 55). 6. food_group corrected from input 'Beverages' to 'Desserts' as this is a dessert dish served with a beverage base, not a standalone beverage. 7. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are closest_parent matches; no exact ontology code exists for this composite dish.
